nativeRange.habitat: Found on coral reefs and rubble areas at depths of roughly 4–65 m, generally observed in pairs or small groups.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical reef-associated marine biotope of the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden.

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding on coral polyps, gorgonians, algae, polychaete worms, and small crustaceans. In aquaria generally accepts a varied diet of dried seaweed, Spirulina-based preparations, vitamin-enriched frozen mysis and brine shrimp, and finely chopped meaty foods. Multiple small daily feedings tend to be best.

summary: The Red Sea Eritrean Butterflyfish (Chaetodon paucifasciatus) is a chevron-patterned species from the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden, generally considered peaceful toward dissimilar tankmates but best kept singly or as a bonded pair and not safe around corals.
careNotes: The Red Sea Eritrean Butterflyfish is endemic to the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden, where it is typically observed in pairs over coral and rubble reef at depths of 4–65 m. In aquaria it has been reported to settle in well when provided ample live rock for grazing and shelter alongside open swimming space. A tank of around 400 L (roughly 100 US gal) is a sensible minimum, as the species is an active swimmer and tends to be territorial toward fish of similar body shape. It is generally considered peaceful toward dissimilar tankmates — such as tangs, wrasses, and cardinalfish — but tends to be aggressive toward other individuals of the same species and toward butterflyfishes with a comparable silhouette. Best kept singly or as a bonded mated pair purchased together. This species is not reef-safe, meaning it should not be kept in an aquarium containing live corals: it has been documented nipping coral polyps, gorgonians (branching colonial animals resembling sea fans), and ornamental worms. Specimens in the aquarium trade are typically wild-collected from the Red Sea.
breedingNotes: In the wild, the Red Sea Eritrean Butterflyfish forms long-term, exclusive pair bonds and spawns by releasing eggs and sperm into open water — a method called broadcast spawning. Captive breeding is not commonly reported.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Retailer husbandry sheet; sourced adult size (5.5"), tank minimum (70 gal), temperament, diet, water parameters (pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, 72–78°F), reef-safe status, compatibility cautions.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Chaetodontidae, Acanthuriformes), max length 14 cm TL, depth 4–65 m, native range Red Sea + Gulf of Aden, wild temperature range 23.6–29.4°C (mean 26.9), diet (coral polyps, gorgonians, algae, worms, crustaceans), monogamous pairing. Used in place of the originally assigned Reef2Reef wiki secondary, which returned HTTP 403.

additional.name: Web search synthesis (LiveAquaria, BlueZoo, theaquariumwiki)
additional.notes: Tertiary cross-check on tank size; sources span 70–100 gal (≈265–379 L). Picked 400 L as the cautious nice-value minimum given active swimming behavior and conspecific aggression.

fish.breedingNotes: Forms exclusive long-term pair bonds in the wild and reproduces by broadcast spawning, releasing eggs and sperm into open water. Captive breeding is not commonly reported.
